(Russian: Глобальная навигационная спутниковая система, romanized: *Global'naya Navigatsionnaya Sputnikovaya Sistema*),[[1]](#cite_note-1) usually referred to by the [acronym](/wiki/Acronym "Acronym") **GLONASS** (**ГЛОНАСС**, IPA: [[ɡɫɐˈnas]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Russian "Help:IPA/Russian")), is a Russian [satellite navigation](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") system operating as part of a [radionavigation-satellite service](/wiki/Radionavigation-satellite_service "Radionavigation-satellite service"). It provides an alternative to the [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") (GPS) and was the second navigational system in operation with global coverage and of comparable precision.

The **Global Navigation Satellite System** (Russian: Глобальная навигационная спутниковая система, romanized: *Global'naya Navigatsionnaya Sputnikovaya Sistema*),[[1]](#cite_note-1) usually referred to by the [acronym](/wiki/Acronym "Acronym") **GLONASS** (**ГЛОНАСС**, IPA: [[ɡɫɐˈnas]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Russian "Help:IPA/Russian")), is a Russian [satellite navigation](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") system operating as part of a [radionavigation-satellite service](/wiki/Radionavigation-satellite_service "Radionavigation-satellite service"). It provides an alternative to the [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") (GPS) and was the second navigational system in operation with global coverage and of comparable precision.

[Satellite navigation devices](/wiki/Satellite_navigation_device "Satellite navigation device") supporting both GPS and GLONASS have more satellites available, meaning positions can be fixed more quickly and accurately, especially in built-up areas where buildings may obscure the view to some satellites.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Owing to its higher [orbital inclination](/wiki/Orbital_inclination "Orbital inclination"), GLONASS supplementation of GPS systems also improves positioning in [high latitudes](/wiki/High_latitude "High latitude") (near the poles).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Development of GLONASS began in the Soviet Union in 1976. Beginning on 12 October 1982, numerous rocket launches added satellites to the system until the completion of the [constellation](/wiki/Satellite_constellation "Satellite constellation") in 1995. In 2001, after a decline in capacity during the late 1990s, the restoration of the system was made a government priority, and funding increased substantially. GLONASS is the most expensive program of [Roscosmos](/wiki/Roscosmos "Roscosmos"), consuming a third of its budget in 2010.

By 2010, GLONASS had achieved full coverage of [Russia's territory](/wiki/Geography_of_Russia "Geography of Russia"). In October 2011, the full orbital constellation of 24 satellites was restored, enabling full global coverage. The GLONASS satellites' designs have undergone several upgrades, with the latest version, [GLONASS-K2](/wiki/GLONASS-K2 "GLONASS-K2"), launched in 2023.[[6]](#cite_note-tsr-20221219-6)

## System description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: System description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Comparison_satellite_navigation_orbits.svg/250px-Comparison_satellite_navigation_orbits.svg.png)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b4/Comparison_satellite_navigation_orbits.svg)

Clickable image, highlighting [medium altitude orbits around Earth](/wiki/Medium_Earth_Orbit "Medium Earth Orbit"),[[a]](#cite_note-7) from [Low Earth](/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit "Low Earth orbit") to the lowest [High Earth orbit](/wiki/High_Earth_orbit "High Earth orbit") ([geostationary orbit](/wiki/Geostationary_Earth_orbit "Geostationary Earth orbit") and its [graveyard orbit](/wiki/Graveyard_orbit "Graveyard orbit"), at one ninth of the [Moon's orbital distance](/wiki/Lunar_distance "Lunar distance")),[[b]](#cite_note-8) with the [Van Allen radiation belts](/wiki/Van_Allen_radiation_belt "Van Allen radiation belt") and the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") to scale

GLONASS is a global navigation satellite system, providing real time position and velocity determination for military and civilian users. The satellites are located in middle circular orbit at 19,100 km (11,900 mi) altitude with a 64.8° inclination and an orbital period of 11 hours and 16 minutes (every 17 revolutions, done in 8 sidereal days, [a satellite passes over the same location](/wiki/Satellite_revisit_period "Satellite revisit period")[[7]](#cite_note-9)).[[8]](#cite_note-ato_glonass-10)[[9]](#cite_note-federal_targeted_program_2002-11) GLONASS's orbit makes it especially suited for usage in high latitudes (north or south), where getting a [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS") signal can be problematic.[[10]](#cite_note-harvey_military-12)[[11]](#cite_note-moskvitch-13)

The constellation operates in three orbital planes, with eight evenly spaced satellites on each.[[9]](#cite_note-federal_targeted_program_2002-11) A fully operational constellation with global coverage consists of 24 satellites, while 18 satellites are necessary for covering the territory of Russia. To get a position fix the receiver must be in the range of at least four satellites.[[8]](#cite_note-ato_glonass-10)

### Signal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Signal")]

#### FDMA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: FDMA")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Glonass-receiver.jpg/330px-Glonass-receiver.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glonass-receiver.jpg)

A combined GLONASS/GPS receiver, ruggedised for the Russian military, 2003

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/GLONASS_GPS_Personal_Radio_Beacon.jpg/330px-GLONASS_GPS_Personal_Radio_Beacon.jpg)](/wiki/File:GLONASS_GPS_Personal_Radio_Beacon.jpg)

A combined GLONASS/GPS Personal Radio Beacon

GLONASS satellites transmit two types of signals: open standard-precision signal L1OF/L2OF, and [obfuscated](/wiki/Obfuscated "Obfuscated") high-precision signal L1SF/L2SF.

The signals use similar [DSSS](/wiki/Direct-sequence_spread_spectrum "Direct-sequence spread spectrum") encoding and [binary phase-shift keying](/wiki/Phase-shift_keying "Phase-shift keying") (BPSK) modulation as in GPS signals. All GLONASS satellites transmit the same code as their standard-precision signal; however each transmits on a different frequency using a 15-channel [frequency-division multiple access](/wiki/Frequency-division_multiple_access "Frequency-division multiple access") (FDMA) technique spanning either side from 1602.0 [MHz](/wiki/Hertz "Hertz"), known as the L1 band. The center frequency is 1602 MHz + *n* × 0.5625 MHz, where *n* is a satellite's frequency channel number (*n*=−6,...,0,...,6, previously *n*=0,...,13). Signals are transmitted in a 38° cone, using right-hand [circular polarization](/wiki/Circular_polarization "Circular polarization"), at an [EIRP](/wiki/Effective_radiated_power "Effective radiated power") between 25 and 27 [dBW](/wiki/Decibel_watt "Decibel watt") (316 to 500 watts). Note that the 24-satellite constellation is accommodated with only 15 channels by using identical frequency channels to support [antipodal](/wiki/Antipodal_point "Antipodal point") (opposite side of planet in orbit) satellite pairs, as these satellites are never both in view of an Earth-based user at the same time.

The L2 band signals use the same FDMA as the L1 band signals, but transmit straddling 1246 MHz with the center frequency 1246 MHz + *n* × 0.4375 MHz, where *n* spans the same range as for L1.[[12]](#cite_note-glonass_xmtr-14) In the original GLONASS design, only obfuscated high-precision signal was broadcast in the L2 band, but starting with GLONASS-M, an additional civil reference signal L2OF is broadcast with an identical standard-precision code to the L1OF signal.

The open standard-precision signal is generated with [modulo-2 addition](/wiki/XOR_gate "XOR gate") (XOR) of 511 kbit/s pseudo-random ranging code, 50 bit/s navigation message, and an auxiliary 100 Hz [meander](/wiki/Meander_(mathematics) "Meander (mathematics)") sequence ([Manchester code](/wiki/Manchester_code "Manchester code")), all generated using a single time/frequency oscillator. The pseudo-random code is generated with a 9-stage shift register operating with a period of 1 [milliseconds](/wiki/Millisecond "Millisecond").

The navigational message is modulated at 50 bits per second. The superframe of the open signal is 7500 bits long and consists of 5 frames of 30 seconds, taking 150 seconds (2.5 minutes) to transmit the continuous message. Each frame is 1500 bits long and consists of 15 strings of 100 bits (2 seconds for each string), with 85 bits (1.7 seconds) for data and check-sum bits, and 15 bits (0.3 seconds) for time mark. Strings 1-4 provide immediate data for the transmitting satellite, and are repeated every frame; the data include [ephemeris](/wiki/Ephemeris "Ephemeris"), clock and frequency offsets, and satellite status. Strings 5-15 provide non-immediate data (i.e. [almanac](/wiki/Almanac "Almanac")) for each satellite in the constellation, with frames I-IV each describing five satellites, and frame V describing remaining four satellites.

The ephemerides are updated every 30 minutes using data from the Ground Control segment; they use [Earth Centred Earth Fixed](/wiki/ECEF "ECEF") (ECEF) Cartesian coordinates in position and velocity, and include lunisolar acceleration parameters. The almanac uses modified [orbital elements](/wiki/Orbital_elements "Orbital elements") (Keplerian elements) and is updated daily.

The more accurate high-precision signal is available for authorized users, such as the Russian military, yet unlike the United States P(Y) code, which is modulated by an encrypting W code, the GLONASS restricted-use codes are broadcast in the clear using only *[security through obscurity](/wiki/Security_through_obscurity "Security through obscurity")*. The details of the high-precision signal have not been disclosed. The modulation (and therefore the tracking strategy) of the data bits on the L2SF code has recently changed from unmodulated to 250 bit/s burst at random intervals. The L1SF code is modulated by the navigation data at 50 bit/s without a [Manchester meander code](/wiki/Manchester_code "Manchester code").

The high-precision signal is broadcast in phase quadrature with the standard-precision signal, effectively sharing the same carrier wave, but with a ten-times-higher bandwidth than the open signal. The message format of the high-precision signal remains unpublished, although attempts at reverse-engineering indicate that the superframe is composed of 72 frames, each containing 5 strings of 100 bits and taking 10 seconds to transmit, with total length of 36 000 bits or 720 seconds (12 minutes) for the whole navigational message. The additional data are seemingly allocated to critical [Lunisolar](/wiki/Lunisolar_calendar "Lunisolar calendar") acceleration parameters and clock correction terms.

##### Accuracy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Accuracy")]

At peak efficiency, the standard-precision signal offers horizontal positioning accuracy within 5–10 metres, vertical positioning within 15 m (49 ft), a velocity vector measuring within 100 mm/s (3.9 in/s), and timing within 200 [nanoseconds](/wiki/Nanosecond "Nanosecond"), all based on measurements from four first-generation satellites simultaneously;[[13]](#cite_note-miller_2000-15) newer satellites such as GLONASS-M improve on this.

GLONASS uses a coordinate [datum](/wiki/Geodetic_datum "Geodetic datum") named "[PZ-90](/wiki/PZ-90 "PZ-90")" (Earth Parameters 1990 – Parametry Zemli 1990), in which the precise location of the [North Pole](/wiki/North_Pole "North Pole") is given as an average of its position from 1990 to 1995. This is in contrast to the GPS's coordinate datum, [WGS 84](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System"), which uses the location of the North Pole in 1984. As of 17 September 2007, the PZ-90 datum has been updated to version PZ-90.02 which differ from WGS 84 by less than 400 mm (16 in) in any given direction. Since 31 December 2013, version PZ-90.11 is being broadcast, which is aligned to the [International Terrestrial Reference System and Frame](/wiki/International_Terrestrial_Reference_System_and_Frame "International Terrestrial Reference System and Frame") 2008 at epoch 2011.0 at the centimetre level, but ideally a conversion to ITRF2008 should be done.[[14]](#cite_note-icg8-16)[[15]](#cite_note-17)

#### CDMA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: CDMA")]

Since 2008, new [CDMA](/wiki/Code-division_multiple_access "Code-division multiple access") signals are being researched for use with GLONASS.[[16]](#cite_note-CDMA-18)[[17]](#cite_note-CDMA_report_2007-19)[[18]](#cite_note-CDMA_report_2011-20)[[19]](#cite_note-GNSS2-21)[[20]](#cite_note-CDMA_2011_CGSIG-22)[[21]](#cite_note-CDMA_2011_ICG-23)[[22]](#cite_note-CDMA_2012_ICG-24)[[23]](#cite_note-IGNSS_2013-25)[[24]](#cite_note-ICG_2016-26)

The interface control documents for GLONASS CDMA signals was published in August 2016.[[25]](#cite_note-glonass_icd-27)

According to GLONASS developers, there will be three open and two restricted CDMA signals. The open signal L3OC is centered at 1202.025 MHz and uses BPSK(10) modulation for both data and pilot channels; the ranging code transmits at 10.23 million [chips](/wiki/Chip_(CDMA) "Chip (CDMA)") per second, modulated onto the carrier frequency using QPSK with in-phase data and quadrature pilot. The data is error-coded with 5-bit [Barker code](/wiki/Barker_code "Barker code") and the pilot with 10-bit [Neuman-Hoffman code](/w/index.php?title=Neuman-Hofman_code&action=edit&redlink=1 "Neuman-Hofman code (page does not exist)").[[26]](#cite_note-28)[[27]](#cite_note-29)

Open L1OC and restricted L1SC signals are centered at 1600.995 MHz, and open L2OC and restricted L2SC signals are centered at 1248.06 MHz, overlapping with GLONASS FDMA signals. Open signals L1OC and L2OC use [time-division multiplexing](/wiki/Time-division_multiplexing "Time-division multiplexing") to transmit pilot and data signals, with BPSK(1) modulation for data and BOC(1,1) modulation for pilot; wide-band restricted signals L1SC and L2SC use BOC (5, 2.5) modulation for both data and pilot, transmitted in quadrature phase to the open signals; this places peak signal strength away from the center frequency of narrow-band open signals.[[22]](#cite_note-CDMA_2012_ICG-24)[[28]](#cite_note-GPSWorld_Nov_2011-30)

[Binary phase-shift keying](/wiki/Phase-shift_keying "Phase-shift keying") (BPSK) is used by standard GPS and GLONASS signals. [Binary offset carrier](/wiki/Binary_offset_carrier_modulation "Binary offset carrier modulation") (BOC) is the modulation used by [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)"), [modernized GPS](/wiki/Modernized_GPS "Modernized GPS"), and [BeiDou-2](/wiki/BeiDou "BeiDou").

The navigational message of CDMA signals is transmitted as a sequence of text strings. The message has variable size - each pseudo-frame usually includes six strings and contains [ephemerides](/wiki/Ephemerides "Ephemerides") for the current satellite (string types 10, 11, and 12 in a sequence) and part of the almanac for three satellites (three strings of type 20). To transmit the full almanac for all current 24 satellites, a superframe of 8 pseudo-frames is required. In the future, the superframe will be expanded to 10 pseudo-frames of data to cover full 30 satellites.[[29]](#cite_note-GPSWorld_Apr_2011-31)

The message can also contain [Earth's rotation](/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation") parameters, [ionosphere](/wiki/Ionosphere "Ionosphere") models, long-term orbit parameters for GLONASS satellites, and [COSPAS-SARSAT](/wiki/International_Cospas-Sarsat_Programme "International Cospas-Sarsat Programme") messages. The system time marker is transmitted with each string; [UTC leap second](/wiki/Leap_second "Leap second") correction is achieved by shortening or lengthening (zero-padding) the final string of the day by one second, with abnormal strings being discarded by the receiver.[[29]](#cite_note-GPSWorld_Apr_2011-31)

The strings have a version tag to facilitate [forward compatibility](/wiki/Forward_compatibility "Forward compatibility"): future upgrades to the message format will not break older equipment, which will continue to work by ignoring new data (as long as the constellation still transmits old string types), but up-to-date equipment will be able to use additional information from newer satellites.[[30]](#cite_note-GPSWorld_Nov_2013-32)

The navigational message of the L3OC signal is transmitted at 100 bit/s, with each string of symbols taking 3 seconds (300 bits). A pseudo-frame of 6 strings takes 18 seconds (1800 bits) to transmit. A superframe of 8 pseudo-frames is 14,400 bits long and takes 144 seconds (2 minutes 24 seconds) to transmit the full almanac.

The navigational message of the L1OC signal is transmitted at 100 bit/s. The string is 250 bits long and takes 2.5 seconds to transmit. A pseudo-frame is 1500 bits (15 seconds) long, and a superframe is 12,000 bits or 120 seconds (2 minutes).

L2OC signal does not transmit any navigational message, only the pseudo-range codes:

Enhanced Glonass-K1 and [Glonass-K2](/wiki/GLONASS-K2 "GLONASS-K2") satellites, to be launched from 2023, will feature a full suite of modernized CDMA signals in the existing L1 and L2 bands, which includes L1SC, L1OC, L2SC, and L2OC, as well as the L3OC signal. Glonass-K2 series should gradually replace existing satellites starting from 2023, when Glonass-M launches will cease.[[24]](#cite_note-ICG_2016-26)[[31]](#cite_note-testoyedov-2015-33)

Glonass-KM satellites will be launched by 2025. Additional open signals are being studied for these satellites, based on frequencies and formats used by existing GPS, Galileo, and [Beidou/COMPASS](/wiki/BeiDou "BeiDou") signals:

* open signal L1OCM using BOC(1,1) modulation centered at 1575.42 MHz, similar to [modernized GPS signal L1C](/wiki/GPS_signals#L1C "GPS signals"), Galileo signal E1, and Beidou/COMPASS signal B1C;
* open signal L5OCM using BPSK(10) modulation centered at 1176.45 MHz, similar to the GPS ["Safety of Life" (L5)](/wiki/GPS_signals#L5,_Safety_of_Life "GPS signals"), Galileo signal E5a, and Beidou/COMPASS signal B2a;[[32]](#cite_note-CDMA_2010_insideGNSS-34)
* open signal L3OCM using BPSK(10) modulation centered at 1207.14 MHz, similar to Galileo signal E5b and Beidou/COMPASS signal B2b.[[18]](#cite_note-CDMA_report_2011-20)

Such an arrangement will allow easier and cheaper implementation of multi-standard [GNSS](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") receivers.

With the introduction of CDMA signals, the constellation will be expanded to 30 active satellites by 2025; this may require eventual deprecation of FDMA signals.[[33]](#cite_note-35) The new satellites will be deployed into three additional planes, bringing the total to six planes from the current three—aided by [System for Differential Correction and Monitoring](/wiki/System_for_Differential_Correction_and_Monitoring "System for Differential Correction and Monitoring") ([SDCM](/wiki/MacAdam_ellipse "MacAdam ellipse")), which is a [GNSS augmentation system](/wiki/GNSS_augmentation "GNSS augmentation") based on a network of ground-based control stations and communication satellites [Luch 5A](/wiki/Luch_5A "Luch 5A") and [Luch 5B](/wiki/Luch_5B "Luch 5B").[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-SDCM_2012-37) GLONASS-KM satellites will also use new L3SVI open signal to broadcast Precise Point Positioning (PPP) to deliver GLONASS High Accuracy Services.[[36]](#cite_note-ICG_Nepal_2025-38)

Six additional [Glonass-V](/wiki/GLONASS-K#GLONASS-V "GLONASS-K") satellites, using [Tundra orbit](/wiki/Tundra_orbit "Tundra orbit") in three orbital planes, will be launched starting in 2025;[[6]](#cite_note-tsr-20221219-6) this regional high-orbit segment will offer increased regional availability and 25% improvement in precision over [Eastern Hemisphere](/wiki/Eastern_Hemisphere "Eastern Hemisphere"), similar to Japanese [QZSS](/wiki/Quasi-Zenith_Satellite_System "Quasi-Zenith Satellite System") system and [Beidou-1](/wiki/BeiDou "BeiDou").[[37]](#cite_note-GPSWorld_GlonassV-39) The new satellites will form two ground traces with inclination of 64.8°, eccentricity of 0.072, period of 23.9 hours, and ascending node longitude of 60° and 120°. Glonass-V vehicles are based on Glonass-K platform and will broadcast new CDMA signals only.[[37]](#cite_note-GPSWorld_GlonassV-39) Previously [Molniya orbit](/wiki/Molniya_orbit "Molniya orbit"), [geosynchronous orbit](/wiki/Geosynchronous_orbit "Geosynchronous orbit"), or [inclined orbit](/wiki/Inclined_orbit "Inclined orbit") were also under consideration for the regional segment.[[18]](#cite_note-CDMA_report_2011-20)[[29]](#cite_note-GPSWorld_Apr_2011-31)

Roscosmos also plans to launch up to 240 small size satellites on the [low Earth orbit](/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit "Low Earth orbit") (LEO) to improve signal availability and interference; LEO satellites will have a limited lifespan of 5 years to allow a faster pace of replenishment.[[36]](#cite_note-ICG_Nepal_2025-38)

## Satellites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Satellites")]

See also: [List of GLONASS satellites](/wiki/List_of_GLONASS_satellites "List of GLONASS satellites")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Glonass-K_-_MAKS2015part8-12.jpg/250px-Glonass-K_-_MAKS2015part8-12.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glonass-K_-_MAKS2015part8-12.jpg)

The Glonass-K spacecraft model

The main contractor of the GLONASS program is Joint Stock Company [Information Satellite Systems Reshetnev](/wiki/Information_Satellite_Systems_Reshetnev "Information Satellite Systems Reshetnev") (ISS Reshetnev, formerly called NPO-PM). The company, located in [Zheleznogorsk](/wiki/Zheleznogorsk,_Krasnoyarsk_Krai "Zheleznogorsk, Krasnoyarsk Krai"), is the designer of all GLONASS satellites, in cooperation with the Institute for Space Device Engineering ([ru:РНИИ КП](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%A0%D0%9D%D0%98%D0%98_%D0%9A%D0%9F "ru:РНИИ КП")) and the Russian Institute of Radio Navigation and Time. Serial production of the satellites is accomplished by the company [Production Corporation Polyot](/wiki/Production_Corporation_Polyot "Production Corporation Polyot") in [Omsk](/wiki/Omsk "Omsk").

Over the three decades of development, the satellite designs have gone through numerous improvements, and can be divided into three generations: the original GLONASS (since 1982), GLONASS-M (since 2003) and GLONASS-K (since 2011). Each GLONASS satellite has a [GRAU](/wiki/GRAU "GRAU") designation 11F654, and each of them also has the military "Cosmos-NNNN" designation.[[38]](#cite_note-43)

### First generation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: First generation")]

Main article: [GLONASS (satellite)](/wiki/GLONASS_(satellite) "GLONASS (satellite)")

The true first generation of GLONASS (also called Uragan) satellites were all three-axis stabilized vehicles, generally weighing 1,250 kg (2,760 lb) and were equipped with a modest propulsion system to permit relocation within the constellation. Over time they were upgraded to Block IIa, IIb, and IIv vehicles, with each block containing evolutionary improvements.

Six Block IIa satellites were launched in 1985–1986 with improved time and frequency standards over the prototypes, and increased frequency stability. These spacecraft also demonstrated a 16-month average operational lifetime. Block IIb spacecraft, with a two-year design lifetimes, appeared in 1987, of which a total of 12 were launched, but half were lost in launch vehicle accidents. The six spacecraft that made it to orbit worked well, operating for an average of nearly 22 months.

Block IIv was the most prolific of the first generation. Used exclusively from 1988 to 2000, and continued to be included in launches through 2005, a total of 56 satellites were launched. The design life was three years, however numerous spacecraft exceeded this, with one late model lasting 68 months, nearly double.[[39]](#cite_note-44)

Block II satellites were typically launched three at a time from the [Baikonur Cosmodrome](/wiki/Baikonur_Cosmodrome "Baikonur Cosmodrome") using [Proton-K](/wiki/Proton-K "Proton-K") [Blok-DM2](/wiki/Blok_D "Blok D") or Proton-K [Briz-M](/wiki/Briz_(rocket_stage) "Briz (rocket stage)") boosters. The only exception was when, on two launches, an [Etalon](/wiki/Etalon_(satellite) "Etalon (satellite)") [geodetic reflector satellite](/wiki/Satellite_geodesy "Satellite geodesy") was substituted for a GLONASS satellite.

### Second generation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Second generation")]

Main article: [GLONASS-M](/wiki/GLONASS-M "GLONASS-M")

The second generation of satellites, known as [Glonass-M](/wiki/GLONASS-M "GLONASS-M"), were developed beginning in 1990 and first launched in 2003. These satellites possess a substantially increased lifetime of seven years and weigh slightly more at 1,480 kg (3,260 lb). They are approximately 2.4 m (7 ft 10 in) in diameter and 3.7 m (12 ft) high, with a solar array span of 7.2 m (24 ft) for an electrical power generation capability of 1600 watts at launch. The aft payload structure houses 12 primary antennas for L-band transmissions. Laser corner-cube reflectors are also carried to aid in precise orbit determination and geodetic research. On-board [cesium clocks](/wiki/Caesium_standard "Caesium standard") provide the local clock source. 52 Glonass-M have been produced and launched.

A total of 41 second generation satellites were launched through the end of 2013. As with the previous generation, the second generation spacecraft were launched three at a time using [Proton-K](/wiki/Proton_rocket "Proton rocket") Blok-DM2 or Proton-K Briz-M boosters. Some were launched alone with [Soyuz-2-1b](/wiki/Soyuz-2 "Soyuz-2")/[Fregat](/wiki/Fregat "Fregat").

In July 2015, [ISS Reshetnev](/wiki/Information_Satellite_Systems_Reshetnev "Information Satellite Systems Reshetnev") announced that it had completed the last GLONASS-M (No. 61) spacecraft and it was putting it in storage waiting for launch, along with eight previously built satellites.[[40]](#cite_note-issr-20150730-45)[[41]](#cite_note-itartass-811771-46)

As on 22 September 2017, GLONASS-M No.52 satellite went into operation and the orbital grouping has again increased to 24 space vehicles.[[42]](#cite_note-47)

### Third generation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Third generation")]

Main articles: [GLONASS-K](/wiki/GLONASS-K "GLONASS-K") and [GLONASS-K2](/wiki/GLONASS-K2 "GLONASS-K2")

GLONASS-K is a substantial improvement of the previous generation: it is the first unpressurised GLONASS satellite with a much reduced mass of 750 kg (1,650 lb) versus the 1,450 kg (3,200 lb) of GLONASS-M. It has an operational lifetime of 10 years, compared to the 7-year lifetime of the second generation GLONASS-M. It will transmit more navigation signals to improve the system's accuracy — including new CDMA signals in the L3 and L5 bands, which will use modulation similar to modernized GPS, Galileo, and BeiDou. Glonass-K consist of 26 satellites having satellite index 65-98 and widely used in Russian Military space.[[43]](#cite_note-reshetnev_glonass_history-48)[[44]](#cite_note-49)

The new satellite's advanced equipment—made solely from Russian components — will allow the doubling of GLONASS' accuracy.[[8]](#cite_note-ato_glonass-10) As with the previous satellites, these are 3-axis stabilized, [nadir](/wiki/Nadir "Nadir") pointing with dual solar arrays.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The first GLONASS-K satellite was successfully launched on 26 February 2011.[[43]](#cite_note-reshetnev_glonass_history-48)[[45]](#cite_note-bbc_russia_launches_satellite-50)

Due to their weight reduction, GLONASS-K spacecraft can be launched in pairs from the [Plesetsk Cosmodrome](/wiki/Plesetsk_Cosmodrome "Plesetsk Cosmodrome") launch site using the substantially lower cost [Soyuz-2.1b](/wiki/Soyuz-2 "Soyuz-2") boosters or in six-at-once from the [Baikonur Cosmodrome](/wiki/Baikonur_Cosmodrome "Baikonur Cosmodrome") using Proton-K Briz-M launch vehicles.[[8]](#cite_note-ato_glonass-10)[[9]](#cite_note-federal_targeted_program_2002-11)

## Status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Status")]

### Availability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Availability")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/GPSTest_screenshot_%282025%29.webp/250px-GPSTest_screenshot_%282025%29.webp.png)](/wiki/File:GPSTest_screenshot_(2025).webp)

Screenshot of GPSTest application showing GLONASS satellites usage in [South Tangerang](/wiki/South_Tangerang "South Tangerang"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") (2025)

As of 12 March 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit), the GLONASS constellation status is:[[63]](#cite_note-auto-68)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Total | 28 SC |
| In operation | 24 SC (Glonass-M/K) |
| In commissioning phase | 0 SC |
| In maintenance | 0 SC |
| Under check by the Satellite Prime Contractor | 3 SC |
| Spares | 1 SC |
| In flight tests phase | 0 SC |

The system requires 18 satellites for continuous navigation services covering all of Russia, and 24 satellites to provide services worldwide.[[64]](#cite_note-69) The GLONASS system covers 100% of worldwide territory.

On 2 April 2014, the system experienced a technical failure that resulted in practical unavailability of the navigation signal for around 12 hours.[[65]](#cite_note-izvestia-70)

On 14–15 April 2014, nine GLONASS satellites experienced a technical failure due to software problems.[[66]](#cite_note-novaya_gazeta-71)

On 19 February 2016, three GLONASS satellites experienced a technical failure: the batteries of GLONASS-738 exploded, the batteries of GLONASS-737 were depleted, and GLONASS-736 experienced a stationkeeping failure due to human error during maneuvering. GLONASS-737 and GLONASS-736 were expected to be operational again after maintenance, and one new satellite (GLONASS-751) to replace GLONASS-738 was expected to complete commissioning in early March 2016. The full capacity of the satellite group was expected to be restored in the middle of March 2016.[[67]](#cite_note-72)

After the launching of two new satellites and maintenance of two others, the full capacity of the satellite group was restored.

### Accuracy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Accuracy")]

According to Russian System of Differentional Correction and Monitoring's data, as of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=GLONASS&action=edit), precision of GLONASS navigation definitions (for p=0.95) for latitude and longitude were 4.46–7.38 m (14.6–24.2 ft) with mean number of navigation space vehicles (NSV) equals 7—8 (depending on station). In comparison, the same time precision of GPS [navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation") definitions were 2.00–8.76 m (6 ft 7 in – 28 ft 9 in) with mean number of NSV equals 6—11 (depending on station).

Some modern receivers are able to use both GLONASS and GPS satellites together, providing greatly improved coverage in urban canyons and giving a very fast time to fix due to over 50 satellites being available. In indoor, urban canyon or mountainous areas, accuracy can be greatly improved over using GPS alone. For using both navigation systems simultaneously, precision of GLONASS/GPS navigation definitions were 2.37–4.65 m (7 ft 9 in – 15 ft 3 in) with mean number of NSV equals 14—19 (depends on station).

In May 2009, [Anatoly Perminov](/wiki/Anatoly_Perminov "Anatoly Perminov"), then director of the [Roscosmos](/wiki/Roscosmos "Roscosmos"), stated that actions were undertaken to expand GLONASS's constellation and to improve the [ground segment](/wiki/Ground_segment "Ground segment") to increase the navigation definition of GLONASS to an accuracy of 2.8 m (9 ft 2 in) by 2011.[[68]](#cite_note-pr_rianscience-73) In particular, the latest satellite design, [GLONASS-K](/wiki/GLONASS-K "GLONASS-K") has the ability to double the system's accuracy once introduced. The system's ground segment is also to undergo improvements. As of early 2012, sixteen positioning ground stations are under construction in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") and in the [Antarctic](/wiki/Antarctic "Antarctic") at the [Bellingshausen](/wiki/Bellingshausen_Station "Bellingshausen Station") and [Novolazarevskaya](/wiki/Novolazarevskaya_Station "Novolazarevskaya Station") bases. New stations will be built around the southern hemisphere from [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") to [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"). Together, these improvements are expected to bring GLONASS' accuracy to 0.6 m or better by 2020.[[69]](#cite_note-kramnik_feb2012-74) The setup of a GLONASS receiving station in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") is also now under negotiation.[[70]](#cite_note-75)
